options.text: Only the spinal nerves
All nerves and ganglia outside the CNS
The brain and spinal cord
Only the cranial nerves

quiz_questions.explanation: The PNS consists of all neural structures outside the brain and spinal cord, including cranial nerves, spinal nerves, ganglia, and peripheral sensory receptors that connect the CNS to the rest of the body.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many pairs of cranial nerves are there?

options.text: 24 pairs
12 pairs
31 pairs
10 pairs

quiz_questions.explanation: There are 12 pairs of cranial nerves (CN I through CN XII) that emerge directly from the brain or brainstem and primarily serve structures of the head, neck, and some thoracic and abdominal organs (via the vagus nerve).

options.text: Voluntary skeletal muscle movements
Heart rate and digestion
Glandular secretions only
Involuntary smooth muscle contractions

quiz_questions.explanation: The somatic nervous system is the voluntary division of the PNS that controls skeletal muscle movements through motor neurons, and also carries sensory information from the skin, muscles, and joints to the CNS.

quiz_questions.question_text: The vagus nerve (CN X) is important because it:

options.text: Only controls tongue movement
Is the smallest cranial nerve with minimal function
Has extensive parasympathetic functions affecting the heart, lungs, and GI tract
Controls vision and pupil size

quiz_questions.explanation: The vagus nerve is the longest cranial nerve and the primary parasympathetic nerve, innervating the heart, lungs, and most of the gastrointestinal tract to regulate heart rate, breathing, digestion, and other visceral functions.

options.text: Taste information from the tongue
Motor signals to the muscles that move the eye
Visual sensory information from the retina to the brain
Sound information from the cochlea

quiz_questions.explanation: The optic nerve (CN II) is a purely sensory cranial nerve that transmits visual information from the photoreceptors of the retina to the visual cortex in the occipital lobe for processing and conscious visual perception.

options.text: Smell from the nasal cavity
Hearing and balance information
Vision from both eyes
Sensation from the face and motor control of chewing muscles

quiz_questions.explanation: The trigeminal nerve is the largest cranial nerve, with three sensory branches providing sensation to the entire face (ophthalmic, maxillary, mandibular divisions) and a motor component that innervates the muscles of mastication (chewing).

options.text: Upper limb including the shoulder, arm, forearm, and hand
Muscles of the trunk and abdomen
Muscles of the neck and face
Lower limb and hip region

quiz_questions.explanation: The brachial plexus is a network of nerves formed from the ventral rami of spinal nerves C5-T1 that provides motor and sensory innervation to the entire upper extremity, including the shoulder, arm, forearm, and hand.

options.text: Protective membranes surrounding peripheral nerves
Types of neurotransmitters released at synapses
Clusters of neuron cell bodies located outside the CNS
Bundles of myelinated axons in the CNS

quiz_questions.explanation: Ganglia are clusters of neuron cell bodies found outside the central nervous system, including dorsal root ganglia (containing sensory neuron cell bodies) and autonomic ganglia (containing postganglionic neuron cell bodies).

quiz_questions.question_text: A peripheral nerve is composed of:

options.text: Only sensory receptor cells
A single neuron and its myelin sheath
Brain tissue that extends into the limbs
Bundles of axon fibers (fascicles) wrapped in connective tissue layers

quiz_questions.explanation: A peripheral nerve is a cable-like bundle of many axon fibers organized into fascicles, surrounded by three layers of connective tissue: endoneurium (around individual fibers), perineurium (around fascicles), and epineurium (around the entire nerve).

quiz_questions.question_text: PNS nerves have a greater capacity for regeneration than CNS nerves because:

options.text: PNS nerves do not have myelin sheaths to repair
PNS neurons divide and replicate after injury
Schwann cells form a regeneration tube that guides regrowing axons
Oligodendrocytes in the PNS actively promote regrowth

quiz_questions.explanation: After PNS nerve injury, Schwann cells proliferate and form a band of Büngner (regeneration tube) that provides a physical guide and growth factors for the regrowing axon, enabling regeneration at approximately 1 mm per day.

options.text: A glial cell that supports neurons in the PNS
A sensory neuron that carries signals from peripheral receptors toward the CNS
A neuron located entirely within the CNS that connects other neurons
A neuron that carries motor commands from the CNS to muscles and glands

quiz_questions.explanation: An afferent neuron is a sensory neuron that transmits information from sensory receptors (such as those detecting touch, pain, or temperature) toward the central nervous system for processing; 'afferent' means 'arriving at' the CNS.

options.text: A motor neuron that carries commands from the CNS to effector organs like muscles and glands
A sensory neuron that detects stimuli in the environment
A neuron that only functions in the brain
An interneuron that integrates signals within the spinal cord

quiz_questions.explanation: An efferent neuron is a motor neuron that transmits signals from the central nervous system outward to effector organs (skeletal muscles, smooth muscles, cardiac muscle, or glands) to produce a response; 'efferent' means 'exiting' the CNS.

options.text: A segment of the spinal cord that controls a group of muscles
A type of peripheral nerve plexus
A type of sensory receptor found in the skin
A specific area of skin that is primarily innervated by a single spinal nerve

quiz_questions.explanation: A dermatome is a specific region of skin supplied by sensory fibers from a single spinal nerve root; dermatome maps are used clinically to assess the level of spinal cord injury or nerve root compression based on areas of sensory loss.

options.text: A receptor that detects body position and movement
A sensory receptor that detects painful or potentially damaging stimuli
A receptor that detects changes in blood pressure
A receptor that detects light for vision

quiz_questions.explanation: Nociceptors are free nerve endings that function as pain receptors, detecting potentially harmful stimuli such as extreme temperature, intense pressure, or tissue-damaging chemicals; the term 'noci-' comes from Latin meaning 'harmful' or 'injurious.'

options.text: Pain that moves from one location to another over time
Pain caused by a referral to a specialist physician
Pain from a visceral organ that is perceived as coming from a different, usually somatic, location due to shared nerve pathways
Pain that is imagined by the patient and has no physical cause

quiz_questions.explanation: Referred pain occurs when visceral pain signals converge on the same spinal cord neurons as somatic sensory fibers, causing the brain to mislocalize the pain; a classic example is heart attack pain being felt in the left arm and jaw.

options.text: A complex network of intersecting spinal nerves that recombine and redistribute fibers to form peripheral nerves serving specific body regions
A cluster of neuron cell bodies in the brain
A type of sensory receptor found in joints
A single nerve that runs directly from the spinal cord to a muscle

quiz_questions.explanation: A nerve plexus is a branching network where ventral rami of spinal nerves merge, exchange fibers, and redistribute to form named peripheral nerves; the major plexuses include the cervical, brachial, lumbar, and sacral plexuses.

options.text: Damage to peripheral nerves resulting in symptoms such as numbness, tingling, weakness, or pain, often in the hands and feet
The normal aging process of peripheral nerves
A disease that affects only the brain and spinal cord
An infection of the meninges surrounding the spinal cord

quiz_questions.explanation: Peripheral neuropathy refers to damage or dysfunction of peripheral nerves that causes symptoms including numbness, tingling (paresthesia), burning pain, and muscle weakness, most commonly affecting the extremities; diabetes is the most common cause.

options.text: A sensory receptor that responds to mechanical forces such as touch, pressure, vibration, and stretch
A receptor that detects light energy for vision
A receptor that detects chemical stimuli such as odors and tastes
A receptor that detects changes in temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: Mechanoreceptors are sensory receptors that detect mechanical stimuli including touch (Meissner's corpuscles), deep pressure (Pacinian corpuscles), stretch (muscle spindles), and vibration, converting physical deformation into electrical nerve signals.

options.text: A condition causing sudden hearing loss due to damage to CN VIII
A condition causing sudden, usually unilateral facial paralysis due to dysfunction of the facial nerve (CN VII)
A chronic condition causing progressive weakness of the limbs
A degenerative disease of the optic nerve causing blindness

quiz_questions.explanation: Bell's palsy is an acute condition involving inflammation or compression of the facial nerve (CN VII), resulting in sudden one-sided facial paralysis with inability to close the eye, smile, or control facial muscles on the affected side.

options.text: Pain radiating along the sciatic nerve from the lower back through the buttock and down the leg
Pain and inflammation in the shoulder joint
Numbness in the fingers caused by carpal tunnel syndrome
A headache caused by tension in the neck muscles

quiz_questions.explanation: Sciatica refers to pain that radiates along the path of the sciatic nerve (the largest nerve in the body), typically caused by compression from a herniated disc, bone spur, or spinal stenosis, producing pain from the lower back through the buttock and down the posterior leg.

quiz_questions.question_text: A construction worker falls from scaffolding and injures his brachial plexus on the right side. During the neurological exam, he cannot raise his right arm, flex his elbow, or grip with his right hand, but his legs and left arm function normally. Why is the deficit limited to his right upper limb?

options.text: The injury actually affected his spinal cord but only the right-sided tracts
The brachial plexus sends branches to both arms, so the left arm should also be affected
The brachial plexus only serves the muscles of the chest and abdomen
The brachial plexus (C5-T1) specifically provides all motor and sensory innervation to the upper limb, so damage affects only that extremity

quiz_questions.explanation: The brachial plexus is formed from spinal nerves C5-T1 and is the sole nerve supply to the upper extremity; unilateral damage affects only the ipsilateral arm because each side has its own separate brachial plexus.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 55-year-old diabetic patient reports progressive numbness and burning pain in both feet that has been gradually moving upward toward the ankles over the past year. The physician diagnoses diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Why does this condition typically affect the feet first?

options.text: Because gravity causes sugar to pool in the feet
Because the feet have fewer sensory receptors than other body parts
Because the spinal nerves to the feet are the smallest and most fragile
Because chronic high blood sugar damages the longest peripheral nerves first, and those serving the feet are the longest

quiz_questions.explanation: Diabetic neuropathy follows a 'stocking-glove' pattern because chronically elevated blood glucose damages the longest peripheral nerve fibers first; since the nerves to the feet are the longest in the body, they are most vulnerable, with symptoms progressing proximally as the disease advances.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient presents to the emergency department with left arm and jaw pain during a heart attack. The ER physician explains this is 'referred pain.' A medical student asks why cardiac pain is felt in the arm and jaw rather than the chest. What is the underlying mechanism?

options.text: Psychological stress from the heart attack causes phantom arm pain
Visceral pain fibers from the heart converge on the same spinal cord segments as somatic fibers from the arm and jaw, causing the brain to mislocate the pain source
The heart attack causes blood clots that block circulation to the arm
Pain fibers from the heart travel to the arm before reaching the spinal cord

quiz_questions.explanation: Referred pain occurs because visceral afferent fibers from the heart and somatic afferent fibers from the left arm and jaw converge on the same dorsal horn neurons in the spinal cord (T1-T5), and the brain misinterprets the visceral pain as coming from the more commonly stimulated somatic areas.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient wakes up with sudden drooping on the right side of her face, inability to close her right eye, and drooling from the right corner of her mouth. She is initially terrified she is having a stroke, but the ER physician determines it is Bell's palsy. How can the physician distinguish Bell's palsy from a stroke?

options.text: Bell's palsy always involves arm weakness, while stroke never does

Bell's palsy affects the entire half of the face (including the forehead) because it involves the peripheral facial nerve (CN VII), while a stroke typically spares the forehead due to bilateral cortical innervation
Bell's palsy causes bilateral facial weakness, while stroke causes unilateral weakness
There is no clinical way to distinguish them; only imaging can differentiate

quiz_questions.explanation: In Bell's palsy (peripheral CN VII lesion), the entire half of the face is paralyzed including the forehead because all motor fibers to that side are affected. In a stroke (upper motor neuron lesion), the forehead is typically spared because the forehead muscles receive bilateral cortical innervation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 30-year-old office worker develops numbness and tingling in her thumb, index finger, and middle finger, along with weakness when gripping objects. She reports the symptoms are worse at night. The physician suspects carpal tunnel syndrome. Which peripheral nerve is being compressed?

options.text: Ulnar nerve
Median nerve
Radial nerve
Musculocutaneous nerve

quiz_questions.explanation: Carpal tunnel syndrome results from compression of the median nerve as it passes through the carpal tunnel at the wrist, causing numbness, tingling, and pain in the median nerve distribution (thumb, index, middle, and lateral half of the ring finger) along with weakness of thumb opposition.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physician performs a dermatome assessment on a patient who fell off a ladder. The patient has lost sensation from the nipple line downward but can feel everything above that level. Based on dermatome mapping, at approximately which spinal cord level is the injury?

options.text: L2 (lumbar)
C4 (cervical)
S1 (sacral)
T4 (thoracic)

quiz_questions.explanation: The T4 dermatome corresponds to the nipple line, so loss of sensation from that level downward indicates a spinal cord injury at approximately T4; dermatome maps are essential clinical tools for localizing the level of spinal cord or nerve root damage.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with a herniated lumbar disc reports sharp, shooting pain that radiates from the lower back down through the buttock and the back of the right leg to the foot. The physician diagnoses sciatica. Why does this single disc herniation cause pain along such a long pathway?

options.text: The pain is referred from the disc itself to the leg muscles
The herniated disc damages the entire spinal cord, affecting all lower body nerves
The sciatic nerve is the longest and thickest nerve in the body, so compression of its roots at the lumbar spine causes pain along its entire distribution down the leg
The herniated disc pinches a blood vessel that supplies the entire leg

quiz_questions.explanation: The sciatic nerve (formed from L4-S3 nerve roots) is the largest nerve in the body, running from the lower spine through the buttock and down the entire posterior leg; a herniated disc compressing its roots at the spine causes radiating pain along the nerve's full distribution.

quiz_questions.question_text: A neurologist uses a tuning fork to test vibration sensation on a patient's great toe and finds no response. She then tests the ankle, knee, and hip, finding that vibration is only felt at the hip. This pattern of sensory loss suggests:

options.text: Damage specifically to the sciatic nerve only
A peripheral neuropathy with distal-to-proximal gradient of nerve damage (stocking-glove pattern)
Complete spinal cord transection at the lumbar level
A brain lesion in the somatosensory cortex

quiz_questions.explanation: A distal-to-proximal gradient of sensory loss (worst at the toes and improving toward the trunk) is characteristic of peripheral neuropathy, where the longest nerve fibers are damaged first, creating the classic 'stocking-glove' distribution pattern commonly seen in diabetic neuropathy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A surgeon is performing an operation near the parotid gland and accidentally damages the facial nerve (CN VII). What postoperative deficit would the patient most likely experience?

options.text: Inability to move the tongue for speech and swallowing
Loss of facial muscle movement on the affected side, including inability to smile, close the eye, or raise the eyebrow
Inability to taste food on the back of the tongue
Complete loss of hearing on the affected side

quiz_questions.explanation: The facial nerve (CN VII) passes through the parotid gland and provides motor innervation to all muscles of facial expression; surgical damage results in ipsilateral facial paralysis affecting the ability to smile, close the eye, raise the eyebrow, and control lip movements.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student is learning to perform a cranial nerve examination. When testing CN V (trigeminal), the student lightly touches the patient's forehead, cheek, and jaw with a cotton wisp, and also asks the patient to clench their teeth. What is the student testing with each maneuver?

options.text: The cotton wisp tests the sensory function of all three trigeminal divisions (ophthalmic, maxillary, mandibular), while clenching tests the motor function to the muscles of mastication
The cotton wisp tests pain sensation, while clenching tests the facial nerve (CN VII)
The cotton wisp tests motor function, and clenching tests sensory function of CN V
Both maneuvers test only the motor function of the trigeminal nerve

quiz_questions.explanation: The trigeminal nerve has both sensory and motor components; light touch on the forehead (V1), cheek (V2), and jaw (V3) tests the sensory divisions, while asking the patient to clench evaluates the motor branch that innervates the muscles of mastication (temporalis and masseter).
